title: Revisiting the potential energy surface for [H3N ��� HCl]: An ab initio and density functional theory investigation
abstract:
Revisiting the Potential Energy Surface 
for [H,N =.. 
HCl]: An Ab Initidand 
Density Functional Theory Investigation 
G. CORONGIU, D. ESTRIN," G. MURGIA, L. PAGLIERI, L. PISANI, 
AND G. SUZZI VALLI 
Centro di Ricerca, Sviluppo e Studi Superiori in Sardegna (CRS4), P.O. Box 1048, 
09100 Cagliari, Italy 
J. D. WATTS 
Quantum Theory Project, P.O. Box 11 8435, University of Florida, Gainesville, Florida 3261 1-8435 
E. CLEMENTI+ 
Universite' L. Pasteur, 3 rue de I'Universite', 67084 Strasbourg Cedex, France 
Received November 17, 1994; vevised manuscript received August 9, 1995; accepted August 14, 1995 
ABSTRACT 
Theoretical calculations of the potential energy surface (PES) for the [NH, + HCl] system 
are presented using several standard ab initio methods such as Hartree-Fock (HF), 
second-order Mdller-Plesset perturbation theory (MP2), coupled cluster (CC), complete 
active space self-consistent-field (CASSCF), density functional theory (DFT), and less 
traditional ab initio approaches such as Dirac-Fock four-components and the use of 
effective Hamiltonian techniques, such as the recently proposed K functional. All 
calculations predict a single minimum for the complex, corresponding to a hydrogen- 
bonded structure, confirming early studies. The dynamical and nondynamical 
contributions to the correlation energy are discussed for different cuts of the PES, 
involving different N-Cl distances. The complex has also been characterized by 
performing a full geometry optimization within the HF and DFT schemes; with the latter 
we have performed also the vibrational analysis. The predicted binding energies and 
infrared (IR) spectrum are compared with other theoretical and experimental results. For 
the gas phase, we propose a binding energy of - 5.3 f 0.5 kcal/mol, thus revising the 
experimental value of -8.0 k 2.8 kcal/mol; for the minimum, the predicted N-H and

y Naturales, Universidad de Buenos Aires, Ciudad Universi 
taria, Pab. 11, (1428) Buenos Aires, Argentina. 
'To whom correspondence should be addressed. 
International Journal of Quantum Chemistry, Vol. 59, 119-134 
0 
1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
1996) 
CCC 0020-7608 196 10201 19-1 6 
 CORONGIU ET AL. 
H-C1 distances are 5.91 +- 0.05 and 2.46 +- 0.05 ax., respectively. When the computation 
is done with approximate inclusion of solvent effects (Onsager reaction field), the 
minimum is shifted and it corresponds to the ion pair NH:. 
C1- structure, similar to 
Mulliken’s outer complex. Since the first ab initio computation for the NH,Cl complex is 
the pioneer work in 1967 by E. Clementi, the present work provides us with an 
opportunity to comment on some aspects of the evolution in computational chemistry, 
particularly for energy determinations. We have concluded our comments with the 
invitation to use four-components Fock-Dirac for molecules both with high and low Z 
atoms, rather than the traditional Hartree-Fock and related methods. In other words, we 
are of the opinion that the time is ready in quantum chemistry to switch from the 
Schrodinger to the Dirac representation, due to new developments in computer hardware 
and software. In addition, the use of effective Hamiltonians, like the recently proposed 
” K  functional,” seems to deserve attention, because of their computational simplicity 
and physical reliability in predicting correlation corrections. 0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
Introduction 
he reaction of ammonia and hydrogen chlo- 
T ride in the gas phase has been the subject of 
several studies in the last 30 years [l-131. The 
interest in this system is mainly that it represents a 
simple model for proton transfer reactions, which 
are important for many chemical and biological 
processes. Moreover, in the field of atmospheric 
sciences, this reaction has been considered a proto- 
type system for investigation of particle formation 
from volatile species [14,151. 
Since the first theoretical ab initio prediction of 
the existence of a stable gaseous complex between 
HC1 and NH, [l], the debate on this topic has 
focused particularly on the nature of the stabiliza- 
tion and on the value of the binding energy. A 
number of theoretical and experimental studies 
have been carried out in order to establish the 
geometry of the equilibrium structure (or struc- 
tures) of the complex and hence to define the 
character of the interaction as either ion-pair-like 
(outer complex) or H-bonded-like (inner-complex) 
[l-131. 
In 1952, when there was no experimental evi- 
dence of a stable NH,C1 gaseous complex, Mul- 
liken suggested that two types of stable structures 
could exist [16]: an inner complex and a more 
stable outer one separated by an energy barrier. To 
test these hypotheses, in 1967 Clementi et al. [l, 21 
performed ab initio SCF-MO calculations and ther- 
modynamical analyses and showed that, within 
the restricted Hartree-Fock (RHF) scheme, a single 
bound complex characterized the potential energy 
surface of HC1 approaching NH, without any bar- 
rier between the separated fragments and the com- 
plex. The computed equilibrium structure was an 
H-bonded species with limited charge-transfer 
character. Although quite advanced for that time, 
the SCF-MO calculations suffered from basis set 
truncation and neglect of correlation effects, and 
therefore energy estimates related to these limita- 
tions were provided, in the spirit of the multi- 
configuration-self-consistent-field (MC-SCF) the- 
ory, which at the time was being discovered. In 
this work, we shall revisit the potential energy 
surface (PES) for the NH, + HC1 process using 
several ab initio computational approaches, includ- 
ing the MC-SCF method, and the currently popu- 
lar density functional theory (DFT) approaches. 
We shall, in addition, use an effective potential 
approach, the newly proposed K functional; fi- 
nally we shall present also computations using the 
relativistic four-component Dirac-Fock method. 
In 1968, Verhaegen and Goldfinger obtained the 
first experimental evidence of complex formation 
from mass spectroscopic studies [3] and proposed 
a Do value between - 7 /  - 13 kcal/mol compared 
with the value of - 9.5,’ - 14 kcal/mol derived by 
Clementi [23 from the computed D, and zero-point 
energy estimates. Analyzing the infrared (IR) spec- 
tra obtained at 15 K in an N, matrix, Pimentel 
confirmed the H-bonded nature of the complex 
and estimated a value for Do in the range of 
- 10/ - 20 kcal/mol[41. Other ab initio SCF calcu- 
lations were then reported by Kollman [5,61, who 
obtained 0, = -12.6 kcal/mol with STO-3G and 
4-31G basis sets, less flexible than the one used by 
Clementi. A substantial improvement in the theo- 
retical study of this system was presented about 12 
years after Clementi’s work by Raffenetti and 
Phillips who approximated the correlation effects

Theoretical calculations of the potential energy surface (PES) for the [NH, + HCl] system
are presented using several standard ab initio methods such as Hartree-Fock (HF),
second-order Mdller-Plesset perturbation theory (MP2), coupled cluster (CC), complete
active space self-consistent-field (CASSCF), density functional theory (DFT), and less
traditional ab initio approaches such as Dirac-Fock four-components and the use of
effective Hamiltonian techniques, such as the recently proposed K functional. All
calculations predict a single minimum for the complex, corresponding to a hydrogen-
bonded structure, confirming early studies. The dynamical and nondynamical
contributions to the correlation energy are discussed for different cuts of the PES,
involving different N-Cl distances. The complex has also been characterized by
performing a full geometry optimization within the HF and DFT schemes; with the latter
we have performed also the vibrational analysis. The predicted binding energies and
infrared (IR) spectrum are compared with other theoretical and experimental results. For
the gas phase, we propose a binding energy of - 5.3 f 0.5 kcal/mol, thus revising the
experimental value of -8.0 k 2.8 kcal/mol; for the minimum, the predicted N-H and

H-C1 distances are 5.91 +- 0.05 and 2.46 +- 0.05 ax., respectively. When the computation
is done with approximate inclusion of solvent effects (Onsager reaction field), the
minimum is shifted and it corresponds to the ion pair NH:.
Mulliken’s outer complex. Since the first ab initio computation for the NH,Cl complex is
the pioneer work in 1967 by E. Clementi, the present work provides us with an
opportunity to comment on some aspects of the evolution in computational chemistry,
particularly for energy determinations. We have concluded our comments with the
invitation to use four-components Fock-Dirac for molecules both with high and low Z
atoms, rather than the traditional Hartree-Fock and related methods. In other words, we
are of the opinion that the time is ready in quantum chemistry to switch from the
Schrodinger to the Dirac representation, due to new developments in computer hardware
and software. In addition, the use of effective Hamiltonians, like the recently proposed
” K  functional,” seems to deserve attention, because of their computational simplicity
and physical reliability in predicting correlation corrections. 0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
he reaction of ammonia and hydrogen chlo-
T ride in the gas phase has been the subject of
several studies in the last 30 years [l-131. The
interest in this system is mainly that it represents a
simple model for proton transfer reactions, which
are important for many chemical and biological
processes. Moreover, in the field of atmospheric
sciences, this reaction has been considered a proto-
type system for investigation of particle formation
from volatile species [14,151.
Since the first theoretical ab initio prediction of
the existence of a stable gaseous complex between
HC1 and NH, [l], the debate on this topic has
focused particularly on the nature of the stabiliza-
tion and on the value of the binding energy. A
number of theoretical and experimental studies
have been carried out in order to establish the
geometry of the equilibrium structure (or struc-
tures) of the complex and hence to define the
character of the interaction as either ion-pair-like
(outer complex) or H-bonded-like (inner-complex)
In 1952, when there was no experimental evi-
dence of a stable NH,C1 gaseous complex, Mul-
liken suggested that two types of stable structures
could exist [16]: an inner complex and a more
stable outer one separated by an energy barrier. To
test these hypotheses, in 1967 Clementi et al. [l, 21
performed ab initio SCF-MO calculations and ther-
modynamical analyses and showed that, within
the restricted Hartree-Fock (RHF) scheme, a single
bound complex characterized the potential energy
surface of HC1 approaching NH, without any bar-
rier between the separated fragments and the com-
plex. The computed equilibrium structure was an
H-bonded species with limited charge-transfer
character. Although quite advanced for that time,
the SCF-MO calculations suffered from basis set
truncation and neglect of correlation effects, and
therefore energy estimates related to these limita-
tions were provided, in the spirit of the multi-
configuration-self-consistent-field (MC-SCF) the-
ory, which at the time was being discovered. In
this work, we shall revisit the potential energy
surface (PES) for the NH, + HC1 process using
several ab initio computational approaches, includ-
ing the MC-SCF method, and the currently popu-
lar density functional theory (DFT) approaches.
We shall, in addition, use an effective potential
approach, the newly proposed K functional; fi-
nally we shall present also computations using the
relativistic four-component Dirac-Fock method.
In 1968, Verhaegen and Goldfinger obtained the
first experimental evidence of complex formation
from mass spectroscopic studies [3] and proposed
a Do value between - 7 /  - 13 kcal/mol compared
with the value of - 9.5,’ - 14 kcal/mol derived by
Clementi [23 from the computed D, and zero-point
energy estimates. Analyzing the infrared (IR) spec-
tra obtained at 15 K in an N, matrix, Pimentel
confirmed the H-bonded nature of the complex
and estimated a value for Do in the range of
- 10/ - 20 kcal/mol[41. Other ab initio SCF calcu-
lations were then reported by Kollman [5,61, who
obtained 0, = -12.6 kcal/mol with STO-3G and
4-31G basis sets, less flexible than the one used by
Clementi. A substantial improvement in the theo-
retical study of this system was presented about 12
years after Clementi’s work by Raffenetti and
Phillips who approximated the correlation effects
POTENTIAL ENERGY SURFACE FOR [H,N ... NCI]
via an SD-CI treatment of the valence electrons [ 71.
This work too proposed a single minimum corre-
sponding to a flexible C,, structure described by
the authors as Mulliken’s outer complex in which
the interaction is dominated by the electrostatic
dipole-dipole attraction but without a strong
charge transfer. These authors obtained a value for
D, of -9.0 kcal/mol and, after including the
zero-point energy computed from harmonic fre-
quencies, a value of Do of -5.9 kcal/mol. Subse-
quently Brciz et al. [9], Latajka et al. [lo], and
Jasien et al. [131 employed different perturbative
post-SCF schemes (MP2, MP3, CEPA, and ACCD)
with basis sets of DZ +pol type. Their results do
not differ substantially from those of Raffenetti
and Phillips and the value of Do they calculated
falls in the range -9/ - 12 kcal/mol; the com-
puted complex nature is H-bond-like.
Since the first theoretical study, due to the
bond-breaking/bond-forming nature of the pro-
cess, the need for a multiconfiguration description
for an exploration of the whole surface has been
recognized 111. The influence of the dynamical
correlation on the equilibrium geometry and on
the stabilization energy of the complex has been
discussed by several authors, and different corre-
lated methods, either perturbational or variational,
were used [7,9,10]. From all these results the
correlated calculations yield similar conclusions
about the stabilization energy of the complex and
the shape of the minimum energy profile (with no
barriers and only one minimum). Nevertheless,
some uncertainties still remain concerning the na-
ture of the interaction and the complexation en-
ergy. Moreover, although some of the previous
studies report calculations on a wide portion of the
surface [7,91, the analysis had been mainly concen-
trated on the equilibrium properties of the com-
plex. Finally, recalling that computational tech-
niques developed for solid-state problems related
to the correlation energy, such as DFT methodolo-
gies, have been rediscovered by the quantum
chemistry community in the last few years [17,181,
we have an opportunity to compare these methods
and other novel methods like the K functional
[ 191, a follower of the Coulomb-Hole Hartree-Fock
approach [20-221, and with an extensive number
In the present work we have computed the
potential energy surface for the collinear C,, ap-
proach with ab initio methods (SCF/MP2,
CASSCF, MR-CASSCF, CC), DFT methods (the
latter both with the local density (LDA) and gener-
alized gradient approximation (GGA), and
with four-component Dirac-Fock. In addition, we
carried out geometry optimization at the mini-
mum and a harmonic vibrational analysis based
on DFT normal mode calculations. Finally, we
have preliminarily explored the effect of solvent
for the reaction in aqueouslike solutions.
This study is organized in three sections. After
this introductory part, in the second section we
provide technical details on the computations. In
the third section the ab initio, the DFT and other
post-Hartree-Fock methods are compared: The
characteristics of the PES as well as the differences
between the methods are discussed; thereafter the
DFT normal mode analysis is presented and com-
pared with previous computational and experi-
The Hartree-Fock (HF) and second-order
Mdller-Plesset (MP2) computations have been
carried out with the MOLECOLE program [23];
the complete active space self-consistent field
(CASSCF) computations have been performed with
the MOLCAS package [24]; for the DFT computa-
tions we have used the MOLECOLE-DFT package
[25]; for the coupled cluster (CC) we have used the
ACES I1 program [26]; for the K functional and for
the four-component Dirac-Fock we have used the
For the HF, MP2, and CC calculations we have
used a rather large basis set, namely a (17, 12, 3,
1/13, 9, 2, l), (14, 9, 3, 1/10, 6, 2, 11, and
(8,3,1/5,2,1) basis set for chlorine, nitrogen, and
hydrogen, respectively. This basis set is marked B
in the tables. With spherical harmonic d and f
functions, this set contains 166 contracted func-
tions, and with Cartesian functions it contains 180
functions. A larger basis set of 380 Cartesian func-
tions has been used to obtain the HF and MP2
limits (see third section). A smaller basis set has
been used to compute Dirac-Fock (DF) functions
with and without an effective potential, the so-
called K-functional technique (see third section), to
account for correlation correction. For the HF + K
functional, DF, and DF + K-functional calcula-
tions, we have used a (16s/13p/2d), (13s,9p,2d),
and (6s, 1 p )  basis set for chlorine, nitrogen, and
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF QUANTUM CHEMISTRY
hydrogen, respectively. The functions were not
contracted. This basis set is marked as A in the
tables. We recall that in DF calculations, the basis
functions for the small components are obtained
by applying the kinetic balance condition to the
functions for the large components (see third sec-
For the CASSCF calculations we have selected a
subspace of 18 molecular orbitals, further parti-
tioned into 6 inactive orbitals, which are doubly
occupied in all complete active space (CAS) config-
urations, and 12 active orbitals for which double,
single, and zero occupation is allowed within each
configuration. The active space has been restricted
to the orbital subspace that strongly contributes to
static correlation due to near degeneracy and rear-
rangement of electrons within partly filled shells.
The active molecular orbitals, which accommodate
16 electrons, are those derived from the 2s, 2 p
orbitals of nitrogen, the four I s  orbitals of the
hydrogens, and the 3s, 3 p orbitals of chlorine. The
Is, 2s, and 2p orbitals of chlorine and the 1s
orbital of nitrogen are in the inactive space. A
Gaussian AN0 (atomic natural orbitals) basis set
[30,311 has been chosen with starting primitive
for nitrogen, and 17s/12p/5d/4f
general contraction scheme has been used, generat-
basis set for chlorine. This basis set
is marked C in the tables. The full configuration
interaction (FCI) expansion on the active space
generates 35,793 configurations. The same basis set
has been used for MR-CASSCF computations.
The DFT calculations have been carried out
with the MOLECOLE-DFT program [251; the
Koh-Sham self-consistent procedure [ 321 is ap-
plied to obtain the electronic density and energy.
One Gaussian basis set is used for the expansion
of the one electron orbitals and a second one for
the electronic density [ 251. Matrix elements of the
exchange-correlation potential are evaluated by a
numerical integration scheme based on the grids
and quadratures reported by Becke [33]. In the SCF
procedure we have used a grid with 30 radial
shells for the chlorine atom, 25 radial shells for the
nitrogen atom, 20 for the hydrogen atoms, and 50
angular points per shell for performing the numer-
ical integration of the exchange-correlation poten-
tial matrix elements. At the end of the SCF pro-
cedure, the exchange-correlation energy and its
contribution to the gradients have been evaluated
using an augmented grid, with 40 radial shells for
the chlorine atom, 35 radial shells for the nitrogen,
30 for the hydrogen atoms, and 194 angular points
per radial shell. A triple Z (GEOTRIPLE Z) plus
polarization orbital basis sets have been used [34];
the contraction pattern is (81111 111 1/611111/1)
(712/1) for hydrogen. The electronic density ex-
pansion basis sets have been derived from the
orbital basis sets. The restricted SCF procedure has
been applied, and in all cases no symmetry has
been imposed on the systems. The computations
have been performed at two different levels of the
density functional theory, the local density approx-
imation (LDA) [ 351 and the generalized gradient
approximation (GGA) [36]. A more detailed de-
scription of the technical aspects of the program is
Coupled-cluster (CC) calculations were carried
out with the ACES I1 program system [26,371.
The CC methods used were the CC singles-and-
doubles (CCSD) [381, CCSD + T(CCSD) [391, and
CCSD(T) [40] methods. CCSD contains all single
and double excitations and their disconnected
products and is therefore exact for two electrons.
The CCSD + T(CCSD) and CCSD(T) methods aug-
ment CCSD with noniterative estimates of the ef-
fect of connected triple excitations using the CCSD
single and double excitation amplitudes. CCSD +
T(CCSD) includes the fourth-order triple excitation
energy, while CCSD(T) adds one fifth-order term
to CCSD + T(CCSD). In the CC calculations, basis
set B was used with spherical harmonic d and f
functions, the core electrons [CKl s, 2s, 2p) and
N(1 s)] were not correlated, and C, computational
symmetry was exploited 1411. Most of the CC
calculations were performed on a Cray-YMP, us-
ing 12 million 64-bit words of memory, and an
algorithm based on a full set of transformed MO
integrals. On the Cray-YMP, each CCSNT) calcu-
lation required approximately 2100 seconds of
single-processor CPU time. Some calculations were
performed on an IBM 580 workstation, using 24
million 32-bit words of memory, and an algorithm
in which the contribution of four virtual index MO
integrals is evaluated from integrals in the A 0
basis. This algorithm is more CPU intensive than
the MO integral-based algorithm, but does not
require storage of the four virtual index MO inte-
grals. On this machine, each CCSNT) calculation
required about 21,000 seconds of CPU time.
POTENTIAL ENERGY SURFACE FOR [H,N ... NCI]
Computational Results and Discussion
In order to compute the potential energy surface
for the [H,N ... H ... Cl] system, we have adopted
the scheme used in Clementi’s first theoretical
study [ 11. The internal geometrical parameters of
the ammonia fragment were kept fixed at the
experimental values [42], namely R(N - H) =
1.9162 a.u. and LH - N - H = 106.78 deg. For a
specified N-C1 distance, we calculated the energy
of the supermolecule for different displacements of
the reactive hydrogen atom on the straight line
connecting the nitrogen atom and the chlorine atom
within C,, symmetry. In this way the values for
the N-H and H-C1 distance define fully the geom-
etry of the complex. This procedure was repeated
for 10 different constant values of N-C1 with HF,
MP2, CASSCF, and DFT methods. A picture of the
geometrical arrangement of the system is given in
Figure 1. The N-C1 distance values for the 10 cuts
of the surface are listed in Figure 2 where the
CASSCF potential energy curves are reported, only
as an example. Note that, when the N-H distance
is relatively large (cases A, B, C, and Z), the active
space selected is insufficient for a correct determi-
nation of the barrier, a quantity in which there is
not much interest, unless we are close to the mini-
mum (cases D, E, F, G, and HI.
If we look at the characteristics of the whole
surface, comparing results obtained with different
methods we notice differences particularly in the
high-energy regions. These are mainly due to the
effect of the correlation and the need of a multi-
configurational description in the region in which
the H-C1 bond is dissociating [l]. The electronic
structure of the two main descriptions of the pro-
FIGURE 1. Geometrical arrangement of the system
FIGURE 2. CASSCF potential energy curves: Each
curve corresponds to a fixed N -CI distance. Values of
distance d(N -CI) characterizing each curve are also
cess, namely [NH, + HCl] and [NH,f+ C1-I, is
clearly of closed-shell type, correctly described by
a single-determinantal wave function. On the other
hand, for long N-C1 distances, the first part of the
migration of the hydrogen atom from C1 to N
proceeds similarly to the case of an isolated H-C1
molecule through the homolytic breaking of the
H-Cl bond, producing a situation in the interme-
diate zone of the transfer which requires a multi-
configurational treatment. Then, when the N-C1
distance is decreased, this zone connects with that
of the NHlC1- closed-shell structure. In Figure 2
this situation is well represented by curve Z which
corresponds to the maximum N-C1 distance ex-
plored (16.41 a.u.). In curves A-C the ”multicon-
figurational” zone is smaller, and in the following
curves the two closed-shell situations connect di-
rectly through smooth decreasing barriers. This
behavior can be understood in terms of the charac-
teristics of the CASSCF wave function, which is
largely dominated by a single configuration (coef-
ficient > 0.99) for any point of the curves D-I
and in the minima zones of curves Z, A-C, while
two configurations assume similar weight in the
intermediate zone of Z, A-C curves. In the last
case the configuration corresponding to the gHCI
+ ascl double excitation becomes as important as
the fundamental closed-shell configuration. So,
while the whole low-energy part of the surface is
of closed-shell nature ad the full complexation
process can be correctly described by a single
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF QUANTUM CHEMISTRY
determinantal wave function, the role of the non-
dynamic correlation becomes very important for
the ” proton-transfer zone” of the surface.
Figure 3 shows the surface cuts in the case of
DFT/GGA calculations, a qualitatively similar pic-
ture could be shown for the DFT/LDA and for
SCF/MP2 results. The shape of curves D-I of
Figure 3 resembles closely the shape found for the
CASSCF case; these are actually the curves for
which the CASSCF wave function is always single
determinantal. For larger N-C1 distances (curves C
and B )  both the DFT approximations as well as
the SCF/MP2 calculations do not reproduce the
flattening of the intermediate (open-shell) zone,
and the two limit closed-shell structures are con-
nected through an increasingly higher barrier.
Then, when the N-C1 distance is further increased
(curves A and Z in Fig. 21, the difficulties of these
methods for describing structures more and more
multideterminantal grow to the point that conver-
gence problems arise preventing the possibility to
obtain a full undisconnected potential energy
From a qualitative point of view, as far as the
global characteristics of the surface are concerned,
the SCF/MP2 and the DFT methods behave simi-
larly. Nevertheless, it is interesting to look in de-
tail at the results for some of the curves, in order
to show the different ways the methods we used
take into account different aspects of correlation.
In Figures 4 and 5 the energy profiles obtained
with all the methods are reported for curves C and
D, respectively. In order to compare the results,
the curves have been shifted to obtain the same
energy value for all the minima on the right; the
FIGURE 4. C potential energy curve [d(N -CI) = 9.41
a.u.1, from different methods of calculation.
SCF energy values are also reported to show the
As pointed out before, the C cut crosses a zone
of the surface where a multideterminantal descrip-
tion is needed. As can be seen in Figure 4, the
CASSCF curve is the only one that shows the
FIGURE 5. D potential energy curve [d(N -CI) = 8.41
a.u.1, from different methods of calculation. In the
upper-right insert we compare CASSCF curve with
MR-CASSCF for three critical points.
POTENTIAL ENERGY SURFACE FOR [H,N
flattening due to the open-shell character of the
intermediate structures. Both the restricted DFT
(LDA and GGA) and the MP2 approaches fail to
reproduce the flattening in the high-energy zone of
curves A-C and Z. The energy barrier is seen to
be very large in the SCF case, a result already
observed in other calculations [43]; methods in-
cluding correlation effects systematically yield
lower barriers. On the other hand, though the MP2
and DFT curves show a similar shape, the DFT
barriers are in all cases lower than MP2 barriers; it
appears that DFT underestimates the barriers, as
was already seen in other cases [43], and therefore
we might expect that the correct barrier height is
In curve D (Fig. 51, where the N-C1 distance is
1 a.u. shorter than the C case, we have a situation
correctly described by assuming a closed-shell
character for the entire curve. The CASSCF wave
function is in this case always single determinant
dominated and the barrier shows the same shape
as those related to the other methods. Indeed, the
few MR-CASSCF computations we have per-
formed (see insert of Fig. 5) yield essentially the
energy obtained at the CASSCF level. In addition,
it should be noted that the change with respect to
the SCF barrier is very small in the CASSCF case;
this reflects the fact that the CASSCF method in-
cludes only the nondynamical contribution to the
correlation energy, negligible for this curve. On the
other hand, the MP2 calculation shows the energy
lowering due to the dynamical contribution to the
correlation energy. The DFT curve shows again a
lower barrier than the MP2 curve, the correct bar-
rier being, as in the case of curve C, probably
between the MP2 and the DFT results.
We have performed CC computations at the
molecular geometries corresponding to the min-
ima of each potential energy curve, from Z to I
(see Fig. 2). We consider these as ”benchmark
computations” for the NH,C1 surface, and for this
reason the corresponding energies are collected in
We have computed, in addition to the above
techniques, also Dirac-Fock four-component rela-
tivistic functions [27,29] at the minimum position
for the potential curves A to I. As is well known,
relativistic effects become more and more impor-
tant the higher the atomic Z value of the atoms of
a chemical system, since for sufficiently high Z
values the relativistic effects become energetically
more important than the electron-electron
Total energies (a.u.1 obtained for the NH,CI system
at the minimum of the potential energy curves Z
to I using three different levels of the CC
method. The basis set is basis B (5d and 7f)
and core electrons were not correlated.
Coulomb repulsion. It is in addition well known
that relativistic effects become as large as correla-
tion corrections already at Z = 12 [44,45]. Finally,
we recall that the Dirac representation not only
recognizes the need for relativistic corrections for
particles with sufficiently high velocity, relative to
the velocity of light, but explicitly associates a spin
to each electron. Since, on the contrary, the
Schrodinger representation ignores both relativistic
corrections and spin, and since the Dirac small
components vanish for nonrelativistic velocities
and, as a consequence, the Dirac-Fock function
merges with the Hartree-Fock function, it follows
that, at least in principle, there are not too many
compelling reasons to retain the Schrodinger rep-
resentation, which might as well be replaced by
the Dirac proposal. Historically, however, there
were practical obstacles to this course of action,
two in particular: the numerical instability associ-
ated with the solution of the Dirac equations and
the added complexity onne must face in passing
from two components spin-orbital to the four-com-
ponent spinors. The realizatioil that the large and
small components are not independent but cou-
pled (e.g., by the kinetic balance constraints, see
Ref. 27-29, 46-48), the increased computational
performance, demonstrated particularly by the ad-
vent of commercial parallel computers, the avail-
ability as public domain of general Dirac-Fock
(DF) computer programs for molecular systems
[27,46-481, are changing the old paradigms, and
we can consider with realism the practical feasibility to
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF QUANTUM CHEMISTRY
Relativistic Dirac-Fock and Hartree-Fock
interaction energies (kcal / mol) and optimized
geometrical parameters (a.u.1 for the NH,CI complex,
using the A basis set (see text).
drop the Schrodinger representation in favor of the
Dirac representation for all molecular systems, whether
In this spirit we have computed Dirac functions
and energies for the NH,C1 system, around the DF
minimum energy given in Table 11, where we
compare Hartree-Fock and Dirac-Fock results ob-
tained using the code MOREL 1271. It is seen that,
as expected, relativistic effects are quite small for
this molecule, because it does not contain very
heavy atoms. The coupling of the large and the
small components, obtained via the kinetic balance
approximation, essentially doubles the basis set;
further, due to the spin-orbit coupling, the spin
has to be considered explicitly, and this brings
about another factor of 2. Thus, in principle, the
computational time and disk space increase by
Hartree-Fock computation with corresponding ba-
sis set in the large components. On the other hand,
in previous studies [27-291, we have stressed that
many integrals, especially in the small compo-
nents, can be neglected even for high Z atoms.
Thus, this factor is only an upper limit and, for
low Z atoms, the number of integrals (with value
smaller than a preassigned threshold) is not much
larger than in the Hartree-Fock approximation. In
Table I11 CPU time and disk space needed for the
computation are shown; it is seen that the ex-
pected 44 factor is notably reduced (about l.14 for
the disk space and 1.4, for the total CPU time).
With modern parallel computers, the above factors
are becoming less and less of an obstacle.
With strict equivalence, as there is a correlation
error in the Hartree-Fock solutions, so there is a
correlation error in the Dirac-Fock solutions; how-
ever, all we have learned in the past 50 years
about Schrodinger correlation corrections, it can be
transferred to Dirac correlation correction. (Inci-

b~~~ time for one SCF iteration.
‘Total time. 20 SCF iterations are needed to reach the

enough to reach the Dirac-Fock convergence.
dentally, the obvious definition of correlation en-
ergy for the Dirac energy is ”the difference be-
tween four components Dirac-Fock energies, Breit
corrected, and the experimental energy.”)
Since all ab initio post-HF techniques have a
high dependence on the number of basis functions
( = N5 for MP2, higher for CASSCF, CI, MRCI, and
CC), quantum chemical computations for large
chemical systems are forced to make use of eflec-
tive Hamiltonians, either those leading to semi-
empirical methods or those which are density de-
pendent (like the DFT approximations), or, finally,
those which are density independent, like the
cent J and K functionals [191. In the MOREL code,
we have the option to call for the implementation
of the ” K-functional technique,” which in essence
associates an exchange-type correction for all elec-
tron pairs with opposite spins, while retaining the
standard exchange terms for electron pairs with
parallel spins [19]. The reliability of the K func-
tional has been demonstrated by the good agree-
ment obtained in computations of closed-shell
atoms with low and high Z (He,Ne,Ar,Kr,Xe),
for open-shell atoms (I and second period) and for
Thus, molecular relativistic codes are avail-
able; these are not much more expensive than
Hartree-Fock codes. Some, like MOREL, have im-
plemented functionals to account for the correla-
tion energy. Finally, there are already efforts to

Binding Energy and Geometry for the
Whereas all computational results previously
mentioned agree in predicting the rather shallow
minimum for the NH,C1 complex as given in
Figure 6, in substantial agreement with laboratory
experiments [3,41, the quantitative determination
of the complex binding energy deserves more
attention. Note that the experimental error bracket
is rather large, about 25% of the total; the complex
weak, the binding energy is a few kilocalories per
mole; the system is not trivially small; all these
factors contribute to demand a rather accurate
computation. In Table IV we collect the binding
energies computed at the minimum and we report
a characterization of the molecular geometry, giv-
ing the two bond lengths, N-H and H-C1. First,
we comment on the simplest approximation, the
HF energies, from approximate HF to HF near to
its energetic limit. The very early work [ 1,2], per-
formed with a double zeta plus polarization basis
set, yields an upper limit to the binding of -19.0
kcal/mol; estimates of the zero-point energy cor-
rection and basis set corrections yield a reduced
value in the range of -9.51/ - 14.00 kcal/mol[2],
this rather large error range being due to different
estimates of the zero-point and the magnitude of
the correlation correction. The lowest total energy
computed was - 515.83052 a.u. for N-H and H-C1
distances of 5.41 and 3.10 a.u., respectively (due to
the computational limitations of the time, 1966, the
geometry optimization was performed in steps of
1 a.u., thus reducing the predictive reliability). The
present HF computations, given in Table IV, uti-
lize an intermediate size basis set, marked A (155
uncontracted functions), a large Gaussian basis set,
marked B (242 functions contracted to 180), and an
even larger AN0 basis set, marked C (328 func-
tions contracted to 1241, yielding at the minimum
the total energies - 516.33597, - 516.34007, and
- 516.34215 a.u., respectively. For these basis, the
basis set superposition error is in the range 0.6 k
0.1 kcal/mol, therefore not very small, relative to a
binding energy ranging from -5.1
kcal/mol. This situation becomes even more un-
pleasant by considering the zero-point energy,
which reduces the HF binding into the range
- 2.6/ - 3.7 kcal/mol with a molecular geometry
with N-H and H-C1 separations of 6.33 k 0.07
and 2.43 k 0.01 a.u., respectively. This binding is
low but not too far from the HF binding of other
H-bond complexes, for examples (H,O),. To have
a more definitive HF value, we have decided to
extend even more the basis set attempting to be
very near to the HF limit. The selected basis set,
after experimentation, totals to 380 uncontracted
functions, and is as follows: for the H atoms (lOs,

brings about redundancy in the basis set. As it is
well known, redundant basis set can have serious
repercussions in the SCF convergency, and this
problem is solved by removing from the diagonal-
ization unwanted basis. In our case 27 basis sets
were removed at equilibrium for the complex
NH,Cl. Different removals in the fragments, rela-
tive to the full complex, makes essential the use of
the counter-poised technique since the basis set
removal in the fragments at infinite separation can
be notably different from the removal in the frag-
ment computation at equilibrium.
Next, in Table IV, we report post-HF literature

are variational computations, but also because no
superposition error has been subtracted. Note that
these post-HF computations predict a somewhat
more compact complex, with an N-C1 distance of

and H-C1 distances of 5.91 k 0.05 and 2.46 k 0.05

Theoretical vibrational analysis on hydrogen-
bonded complexes is a valuable tool for extracting
information from the potential energy surfaces of
these systems. Moreover, the computed values for
infrared frequencies are readily compared with
experimental ones, allowing a test of the accuracy
In this work we have performed a harmonic
vibrational analysis of the NH,C1 system, by using
DFT. As is well known, DFT is capable of provid-
ing good values for vibrational frequencies and IR
intensities for a large class of organic and inor-
ganic systems, attaining a quality comparable to
that of other post-HF methods, but at a consider-
ably lower computational cost, and without the
need for scaling procedures [17,18]. Vibrational
frequencies have been calculated by evaluating the
second derivatives of the energy with respect to
the nuclear positions through a numerical differen-
tiation of the gradients. Infrared absorption inten-
sities have been calculated by taking the numerical
derivatives of the dipole moment and transform-
ing them to the corresponding ones with respect to
the normal modes. Results are shown in Table V,
together with some of the available experimental
The first observation of the IR NH,C1 spectrum
is due to Ault and Pimentel [41 who observed in
an N, matrix a H-C1 stretching frequency of 705
cm-' and a H-C1 bending frequency of 605 cm-l.
Later, Barnes et al. [8] reported a more complete
set of fundamentals, observed in Ar and N, matri-
ces; in that work it was shown that the IR spec-
trum of the NH,C1 complex is extremely sensitive
to the environment, and that the enormous shifts
observed in the H-C1 stretching frequency upon
complexation are partly due to proton transfer

In Table V, the predicted values for the isolated

is satisfactory, the exact agreements being acci-

Frequencies calculated for NH,Cl are reported
on the left of Table V. The NH, asymmetric
stretching is slightly affected by complexation; this
can be seen from experimental values and also
from the calculated ones, which, however, show
an opposite trend for the frequencies. Also the

this mode. While asymmetric bending modes of
NH, are not affected by the bonding with HC1, the

seen in the experimental as well as in the calcu-
lated values, and it is also reproduced by the MP2
The shift observed upon complexation in the
HC1 stretching frequency is crucial in this vibra-
tional analysis, since it is directly related to the
position of the hydrogen atom between the N and
C1 atoms and to the shape of the potential energy
surface around it. Experimental values of Ref. 8
report a dramatic shift for the HC1 stretching fre-
quency, showing a decrease larger than 1500 cm-',
when observed in an Ar matrix. This shift is not
reproduced by our DFT calculation, neither by the
MP2 calculation of Ref. 50; Ref. 51 reports a value
for the shift of about 400 cm-', when calculated in
the harmonic approximation, and of about 700
cmpl when anharmonic effects are included with
a CI treatment: Neither of these calculations match
the available experimental data [52,53], confirm-
ing the presence of matrix effects affecting the
results. These are probably the reasons also for the
nonreproducibility of the experimental values for
the intermolecular vibrational modes, with the ex-
ception of the N-HC1 stretching, for which a good
agreement between experimental and calculated
To confirm the strong influence of the environ-

complex, we have performed DFT calculations us-

the self-consistent reaction field (SCRF), to model

We conclude this section with a comparative
note on the theoretical methods used in this inves-

tigation: Large differences can be observed be-

Comments on the Reaction Surface in
The previous sections have been concerned with
the reaction in the gaseous phase. However, it has
long been known that the reaction is strongly in-
fluenced by the presence of water molecules: The
simplest experiment is obtained by the mixing of
HC1 and NH, fumes in the standard air atmo-
In the SCRF method the solvent is modeled as a
continuum characterized by a dielectric constant.
The solute is placed in a spherical cavity, im-
mersed in the continuum. We have chosen a
medium of dielectric constant E = 2 to simulate a
weakly interacting environment. The geometry of
the minimum for DFT was reoptimized, and the
vibrational frequencies were evaluated in the pres-
ence of the reaction field; the results show how
even a weakly interacting environment can have
large effects on the geometrical and vibrational
properties of NH,CI, particularly on the vibra-
tional frequency of H-C1 stretching in the com-
plex, while the corresponding isolated HC1 stretch-
ing frequency remains almost unaltered. The shift
due to the E = 2 environment is -608 cm-' with
LDA and -48 cm-' with GGA, while in the
isolated system a shift of + l  cm-' for both the
density approximations has been obtained.
From the collection of all these results, it seems
reasonable to conclude that a harmonic vibrational
analysis of the CI... H ..- N subsystem in NH,C1
should not agree with the matrix experiment be-
cause of the anharmonic effects (claimed to be
very important [51]) and because of environment

minima, the new one corresponding to Mulliken's
outer complex and with the characterization, from

NH;. C1-. Further, by extending the cavity size,
we can move the two ions farther and farther
apart, and this leads to lower and lower energy:
Therefore, in solution the ion pair is the deepest
energy, but it corresponds to two infinitely sepa-

with the solvent cavity, one without. The shift of

approaches. The accuracy, however, for DFT is at

Thus, with some optimism, we could conclude
that the very long task of computing reasonable
binding energies in computational chemistry is
nearly concluded. Concerning correlation energy,
the (many) thousands-of-determinants, CI-like, ap-
proaches have been revealed to be an impossible
avenue for medium-sized and large molecular sys-
tems, as readily deduced from computational com-
plexity arguments [57]. On the other hand, effec-
tive Hamiltonian approaches have proven to offer
a reasonably accurate and a computationally feasi-
ble approach to medium and large molecular sys-
tems. In the effective Hamiltonian techniques a
constraint is imposed on the wave function which
is confined to have a predetermined complexity (in
practice, either one determinant, as in Refs. 20, 32,
and 57, or very few determinants as in Refs. 19,58,
and 59); the computation of the ”exact energy” is
attempted by introducing effective potentials, ei-
ther explicitly density dependent (as in Refs. 32,
58, and 59) or density independent (as in Refs.
19-22). The exchange energy in the effective
Hamiltonian techniques is either computed cor-
rectly (as in Refs. 19-22, 58, 59, and in all ab initio
methods thus allowing for the computation of ex-
cited states) or is merged into ”exchange and cor-
relation” effective Hamiltonian terms, formally de-
vised (and numerically fitted) for ground-state
wave functions. The traditional post-HF methods
will guide this development, by providing compu-
tational data essential as benchmark and tests, but
none of these methods is or will be capable of
dealing with large molecules in 300-400 atoms
range (excluding untypical cases of high symme-
try). For such systems, one must use either
semiempirical techniques, today at an excellent
state of development [60], or effective Hamiltoni-
ans methods. The advent of parallel computers
and robust codes for post-Dirac methods allow us
to hope that within few years the Dirac-Fock and
post-DF methods will replace the present HF and
post-HF techniques. The early 1960s dream to mas-
ter large molecular systems by accurate ab initio
methods has clearly failed: The effective potential
However, 30 years after the first ab initio com-
putation of a system like NH,Cl, a very different
picture emerges if we plan to use PES to obtain
kinetic and reaction rate data [2]. For molecules
with 6 (or more) atoms and for chemical processes
involving bond formation and bond breaking,
computational chemistry has little new to offer
than was available 30 years ago. This situation is
somewhat discouraging, chemistry being essen-
tially the study of the formation of molecules, for
processes involving bond breaking and bond form-
ing. Perhaps there is an unbalanced effort when
we compare the support for the development and
application of computational methods and tech-
niques aimed at total energy predictions rather
than at kinetic and reaction rates [61].
